subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a combination of a thin plano-concave lens and a thin plano-convex lens is used to image an object placed at infinity. The radius of curvature of both the lenses is 30 cm and refraction index of the material for both the lenses is 1.75. Both the lenses are placed at distance of 40 cm from each other. Due to the combination, the image of the object is formed at distance x= ______ cm, from concave lens.
question: As shown in the figure, a combination of a thin plano-concave lens and a thin plano-convex lens is used to image an object placed at infinity. The radius of curvature of both the lenses is 30 cm and refraction index of the material for both the lenses is 1.75. Both the lenses are placed at distance of 40 cm from each other. Due to the combination, the image of the object is formed at distance x= ______ cm, from concave lens.
solution:
Using Lens Makers formula for the plano-concave part, we can write

1f1=(1.75-1)-130

⇒  f1=-40 cm

Using Lens Makers formula for the plano-convex part, we can write

1f2=1.75-1130⇒ f2=40 cm

Image from first lens will be virtual and on the left of first lens at focal length 40 cm. So the object for second lens will be 80 cm from second lens which is 2f2. Final image is formed at 80 cm from second lens on the right.
So, x=120.

subject: Physics
q:
Which of the following processes play a part in the formation of a rainbow?
(i) Refraction
(ii) Total internal reflection
(iii) Dispersion
(iv) Interference
question:
Which of the following processes play a part in the formation of a rainbow?
(i) Refraction
(ii) Total internal reflection
(iii) Dispersion
(iv) Interference
solution: Rainbow is formed due to the dispersion of light suffering refraction and total internal reflection (TIR) in the droplets present in the atmosphere.

subject: Physics
q: A thin convex lens L (refractive index =1.5 ) is placed on a plane mirror M. When a pin is placed at A, such that OA=18 cm, its real inverted image is formed at A itself, as shown in figure. When liquid of refractive index μl is put between the lens and the mirror, the pin has to be moved to A', such that OA'=27 cm, to get its inverted real image at A' itself. The value of μl will be
question: A thin convex lens L (refractive index =1.5 ) is placed on a plane mirror M. When a pin is placed at A, such that OA=18 cm, its real inverted image is formed at A itself, as shown in figure. When liquid of refractive index μl is put between the lens and the mirror, the pin has to be moved to A', such that OA'=27 cm, to get its inverted real image at A' itself. The value of μl will be
solution:
1f1=12×218
1f2=μ1-1-18
When μ1 is filled between lens and mirror,
P=218-218μ1-1=2-2μ1+218
⇒Fm=-182-μ1
2=6-3μ1
3μ1=4
μ1=43

subject: Physics
q: A thin convex lens made from crown glass <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> <mo>=</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> has focal length <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. When it is measured in two different liquids having refractive indices <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>  and <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>, it has the focal lengths <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>1</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>2</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> respectively. The correct relation between the focal lengths is :
question: A thin convex lens made from crown glass <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> <mo>=</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> has focal length <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. When it is measured in two different liquids having refractive indices <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>  and <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>, it has the focal lengths <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>1</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>2</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> respectively. The correct relation between the focal lengths is :
solution:
Let [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] = x

∴ 1 f = [ 3 2 1 - 1 ] [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] ⇒ f = 2 x

When in liquid 1

1 f 1 = [ 3 2 4 3 - 1 ] [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] ⇒ f 1 = 8 x

When in liquid 2

1 f 2 = [ 3 2 5 3 - 1 ] [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] ⇒ f 2 = - 10 x

subject: Physics
q: Consider light travelling from a medium $A$ to medium $B$ separated by a plane interface. If the light undergoes total internal reflection during its travel from medium $A$ to $B$ and the speed of light in media $A$ and $B$ are $2.4 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $2.7 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively, then the value of critical angle is :
question: Consider light travelling from a medium $A$ to medium $B$ separated by a plane interface. If the light undergoes total internal reflection during its travel from medium $A$ to $B$ and the speed of light in media $A$ and $B$ are $2.4 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $2.7 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively, then the value of critical angle is :
solution:
The refractive index of a medium is inversely proportional to the speed of light in that medium, given by $n = \frac{c}{v}$.
For light travelling from medium $A$ to medium $B$, the critical angle $\theta_c$ is defined when light travels from a denser to a rarer medium.
Given speeds are $v_A = 2.4 \times 10^8$ m/s and $v_B = 2.7 \times 10^8$ m/s.
The refractive indices are $n_A = \frac{c}{v_A}$ and $n_B = \frac{c}{v_B}$.
The critical angle $\theta_c$ is given by $\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_B}{n_A} = \frac{v_A}{v_B}$.
Substituting the values: $\sin \theta_c = \frac{2.4 \times 10^8}{2.7 \times 10^8} = \frac{24}{27} = \frac{8}{9}$.
To find the value in terms of $\tan^{-1}$, we use the trigonometric identity for a right-angled triangle where perpendicular $p = 8$ and hypotenuse $h = 9$.
The base $b = \sqrt{h^2 - p^2} = \sqrt{9^2 - 8^2} = \sqrt{81 - 64} = \sqrt{17}$.
Thus, $\tan \theta_c = \frac{p}{b} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{17}}$.
Therefore, $\theta_c = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{\sqrt{17}}\right)$.

question: To determine refractive index of glass slab using a travelling microscope, minimum number of readings required are :

Reading two ⇒ with slab

subject: Physics
q: When an object is placed 40 cm away from a spherical mirror an image of magnification $\frac{1}{2}$ is produced. To obtain an image with magnification of $\frac{1}{3}$, the object is to be moved :
question: When an object is placed 40 cm away from a spherical mirror an image of magnification $\frac{1}{2}$ is produced. To obtain an image with magnification of $\frac{1}{3}$, the object is to be moved :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{f}-\mathrm{u}} \\ & \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{f}-(-40)} \\ & \mathrm{f}+40=2 \mathrm{f} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}=40 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \text { now } \mathrm{m}=\frac{1}{3}=\frac{40}{40-\mathrm{u}} \\ & 40-\mathrm{u}=120 \Rightarrow \mathrm{u}=-80\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens (of focal length <math><mi> </mi><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) and a concave mirror, having their principal axes along the same lines, are kept <math><mn>80</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart from each other. The concave mirror is to the right of the convex lens. When an object is kept at a distance of <math><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> to the left of the convex lens, its image remains at the same position even if the concave mirror is removed. The maximum distance of the object for which this concave mirror, by itself would produce a virtual image would be:
question: A convex lens (of focal length <math><mi> </mi><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> ) and a concave mirror, having their principal axes along the same lines, are kept <math><mn>80</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart from each other. The concave mirror is to the right of the convex lens. When an object is kept at a distance of <math><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> to the left of the convex lens, its image remains at the same position even if the concave mirror is removed. The maximum distance of the object for which this concave mirror, by itself would produce a virtual image would be:
solution:
Image formed by lens
1v-1u=1f
⇒1v+130=120
⇒1v=120-130
⇒v=60 cm
In the image remains at the same position even if the concave mirror is removed indicates that the image formed by lens is formed at the center of curvature of spherical mirror.
∴ Radius of curvature of mirror =80-60=20 cm
Focal length, f=10 cm
For virtual image to be formed, object is to be kept between focus and pole. Hence, the maximum distance of object from spherical mirror for which virtual image is formed will be its focal length 10cm

subject: Physics
q: We wish to make a microscope with the help of two positive lenses both with a focal length of 20 $\mathrm{mm}$ each and the object is positioned $25 \mathrm{~mm}$ from the objective lens. How far apart the lenses should be so that the final image is formed at infinity?
question: We wish to make a microscope with the help of two positive lenses both with a focal length of 20 $\mathrm{mm}$ each and the object is positioned $25 \mathrm{~mm}$ from the objective lens. How far apart the lenses should be so that the final image is formed at infinity?
solution: To obtain final image at infinity, object which is the image formed by objective should be at focal distance of eye-piece. By lens formula (for objective) $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} \\ \text { or, } \quad & \frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{-25}=\frac{1}{20} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{1}{v_0}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{25}=\frac{5-4}{100}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~mm} \\ \therefore \quad & v_0=100 \mathrm{~mm} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore the distance between the lenses $$ =v_0+f_e=100 \mathrm{~mm}+20 \mathrm{~mm}=120 \mathrm{~mm} $$

To the observer, the tree appears as
question: An observer looks at a distant tree of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with a telescope of magnifying power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math>. To the observer, the tree appears as
solution: Angular magnification of the telescope is 20 so now the tree will subtend a larger angle at the observer's eye and hence appears 20 times nearer

subject: Physics
q: In the diagram given below, there are three lenses formed. Considering negligible thickness of each of them as compared to $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$, i.e., the radii of curvature for upper and lower surfaces of the glass lens, the power of the combination is
question: In the diagram given below, there are three lenses formed. Considering negligible thickness of each of them as compared to $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$, i.e., the radii of curvature for upper and lower surfaces of the glass lens, the power of the combination is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow p_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{p}_1+\mathrm{p}_2+\mathrm{p}_3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_1=\left(\frac{4}{3}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_1=\left(\frac{1}{3\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_2=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_2=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_3=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)=-\frac{1}{3\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right) \\ & =-\frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A container contains a liquid with refractive index of 1.2 up to a height of 60 cm and another liquid having refractive index 1.6 is added to height H above first liquid. If viewed from above, the apparent shift in the position of bottom of container is 40 cm. The value of H is ______ cm.
(Consider liquids are immisible)
question:
A container contains a liquid with refractive index of 1.2 up to a height of 60 cm and another liquid having refractive index 1.6 is added to height H above first liquid. If viewed from above, the apparent shift in the position of bottom of container is 40 cm. The value of H is ______ cm.
(Consider liquids are immisible)
solution:
$y=$ apparent depth of bottom
$\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{H}}{1.6}+\frac{60}{1.2}$
Shift $=40$
$H+60-y=40$
$\mathrm{H}+60-\frac{\mathrm{H}}{1.6}-\frac{60}{1.2}=40$
$\frac{6}{16} \mathrm{H}=30$
$\mathrm{H}=80 \mathrm{~cm}$

solution:
f=D2-d24D=1002-4024(100)=(100+40)(100-40)4(100)=21 cm

P=1f=10021=N100

N=476.19

subject: Physics
q: A finite size object is placed normal to the principal axis at a distance of 30 cm from a convex mirror of focal length 30 cm. A plane mirror is now placed in such a way that the image produced by both the mirrors coincide with each other. The distance between the two mirrors is :
question: A finite size object is placed normal to the principal axis at a distance of 30 cm from a convex mirror of focal length 30 cm. A plane mirror is now placed in such a way that the image produced by both the mirrors coincide with each other. The distance between the two mirrors is :
solution:
For Convex mirror
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{30} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{2}{30}=\frac{1}{15} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=15 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$
Image formed by convex mirror is at 45 cm from object so plane mirror should be placed midway at 22.5 cm from object so that both of their images may coinside,
Therefore distance between both mirrors
$=30-22.5=7.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Correct Answer : Option 2

subject: Physics
q: The same size images are formed by a convex lens when the object is placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> or at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the lens. The focal length of convex lens is
question: The same size images are formed by a convex lens when the object is placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> or at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the lens. The focal length of convex lens is
solution:
m=fu+f

+m=f-10+f    ....1

-m=f-20+f     .....2

1/2

-1=f-20f-10

10-f=f-20

30=2f

f=15 cm

subject: Physics
q: Time taken by light to travel in two different materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of same thickness is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then the thickness of material, in meter is: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are velocities of light in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> materials respectively).
question: Time taken by light to travel in two different materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of same thickness is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then the thickness of material, in meter is: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are velocities of light in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> materials respectively).
solution:
The speed of light in a medium is inversely proportional to the refractive index of the material. Therefore,

μAμB=vBvA=12

Let the thickness is d

⇒t2-t1=dvB-dvA=5×10-10 s

As vA=2vB⇒d=5×10-10×2vB

⇒d=5×10-10×vA m.

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic light wave with wavelength λ1 and frequency ν1 in air enters another medium. If the angle of incidence and angle of refraction at the interface are 45° and 30° respectively, then the wavelength λ2 and frequency ν2 of the refracted wave are:
question: A monochromatic light wave with wavelength λ1 and frequency ν1 in air enters another medium. If the angle of incidence and angle of refraction at the interface are 45° and 30° respectively, then the wavelength λ2 and frequency ν2 of the refracted wave are:
solution:
By using Snell's law, n1sinθ1=n2sinθ2, the refractive index is 

μ=sin45°sin30°=1212=2

Also using the relation n=cv where n is the refractive index, c is the velocity of light in air and v is the velocity in the medium

⇒cair v =2=λ1 λ2

The velocities are given by 

v=νλ2c=νλ1

⇒λ1=2λ2 and ν1=ν2.

The frequency of light does not change when the medium changes.

subject: Physics
q:
An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm from a convex lens. A convex mirror of focal length 15 cm is placed on another side of the lens at 8 cm as shown in the figure. The image of the object coincides with the object.

When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed at a position. The distance of the image from the object will be ___ cm
question:
An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm from a convex lens. A convex mirror of focal length 15 cm is placed on another side of the lens at 8 cm as shown in the figure. The image of the object coincides with the object.

When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed at a position. The distance of the image from the object will be ___ cm
solution: For the object to coincide with the image, the light must fall perpendicularly to the mirror. This means that the light will have to converge at C of the mirror. Without the mirror also, the light would coverage at C. So the distance is:=12+8+30=50 cm.

subject: Physics
q: A plano-convex lens having radius of curvature of first surface 2 cm exhibits focal length of $f_1$ in air. Another plano-convex lens with first surface radius of curvature 3 cm has focal length of $\mathrm{f}_2$ when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.2 . If both the lenses are made of same glass of refractive index 1.5 , the ratio of $f_1$ and $f_2$ will be
question: A plano-convex lens having radius of curvature of first surface 2 cm exhibits focal length of $f_1$ in air. Another plano-convex lens with first surface radius of curvature 3 cm has focal length of $\mathrm{f}_2$ when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.2 . If both the lenses are made of same glass of refractive index 1.5 , the ratio of $f_1$ and $f_2$ will be
solution:
For case (1)
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1.5}{v_1}-\frac{1}{\propto}=\frac{(1.5-1)}{R_1} \\ & \frac{1}{f_1}-\frac{1.5}{v_1}=\frac{1-1.5}{\propto}\end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{(1.5-1)}{R_1}$ ...(1) $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{1}{2 R_1}$ For case (2) $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1.5}{v_1}-\frac{1.2}{\infty}=\frac{(1.5-1.2)}{R_2} \\ & \frac{1.2}{f_2}-\frac{1.5}{v_1}=\frac{(1.2-1.5)}{\propto} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1.2}{f_2}=\frac{0.3}{R_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{4 R_2} \end{aligned}$ So clearly, $\frac{f_1}{f_2}=\frac{2 \times 2}{4 \times 3}=\frac{1}{3}$

subject: Physics
q: A collimated beam of light of diameter 2 mm is propagating along $x$-axis. The beam is required to be expanded in a collimated beam of diameter 14 mm using a system of two convex lenses. If first lens has focal length 40 mm, then the focal length of second lens is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
question: A collimated beam of light of diameter 2 mm is propagating along $x$-axis. The beam is required to be expanded in a collimated beam of diameter 14 mm using a system of two convex lenses. If first lens has focal length 40 mm, then the focal length of second lens is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
solution:
For beam expander using two convex lenses:
Magnification $= \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{f_2}{f_1}$
$\frac{14}{2} = \frac{f_2}{40}$
$f_2 = 7 \times 40 = 280$ mm

subject: Physics
q: In an optics experiment, with the position of the object fixed, a student varies the position of a convex lens and for each position, the screen is adjusted to get a clear image of the object. A graph between the object distance $u$ and the image distance $v$, from the lens, is plotted using the same scale for the two axes. A straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the x-axis meets the experimental curve at $P$. The coordinates of $P$ will be
question: In an optics experiment, with the position of the object fixed, a student varies the position of a convex lens and for each position, the screen is adjusted to get a clear image of the object. A graph between the object distance $u$ and the image distance $v$, from the lens, is plotted using the same scale for the two axes. A straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the x-axis meets the experimental curve at $P$. The coordinates of $P$ will be
solution: It is possible when object kept at centre of curvature. $$ \begin{aligned} & u=v \\ & u=2 f, v=2 f . \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to measure the focal length (f) of a convex lens, the magnitude of object distance(x) and the image distance(y) are measured with reference to the focal point of the lens. The y-x plot is shown in figure. The focal length of the lens is _____cm.
question: In an experiment to measure the focal length (f) of a convex lens, the magnitude of object distance(x) and the image distance(y) are measured with reference to the focal point of the lens. The y-x plot is shown in figure. The focal length of the lens is _____cm.
solution:
From Newton's formula for lens, we can write

x1x2=f2⇒20×20=f2

Therefore, f=20 cm

subject: Physics
q: In parallax method for the determination of focal length of a concave mirror, the object should always be placed:
question: In parallax method for the determination of focal length of a concave mirror, the object should always be placed:
options: between the focus $(F)$ and the centre of curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
between the pole($P$) and the focus $(F)$ of the concave mirror ONLY
beyond the centre of the curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
at any point beyond the focus $(F)$ of the mirror
solution:
In the parallax method for determining the focal length of a concave mirror, we use an object pin and its real image formed by the mirror.
The method of 'no parallax' involves aligning the tip of the object pin with the tip of its real image. This is only possible if the image formed is real.
For a concave mirror, a real image is formed only when the object is placed at any position beyond the focus ($F$), i.e., $u > f$.
If the object is placed between the pole ($P$) and the focus ($F$), the image formed is virtual, and it cannot be caught on a screen or used for the standard parallax alignment with a physical pin in front of the mirror.
Therefore, the object should be placed at any point beyond the focus ($F$) of the mirror to ensure a real image is formed.

subject: Physics
q: A short straight object of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> lies before the central axis of a spherical mirror whose focal length has absolute value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>f</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The image of object produced by the mirror is of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and has the same orientation of the object. One may conclude from the information:
question: A short straight object of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> lies before the central axis of a spherical mirror whose focal length has absolute value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>f</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The image of object produced by the mirror is of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and has the same orientation of the object. One may conclude from the information:
options: Image is real, same side of convex mirror.
Image is virtual, opposite side of concave mirror.
Image is real, same side of concave mirror.
Image is virtual, opposite side of convex mirror.
solution: Since orientation is same image is virtual. Since image is smaller the mirror has to be convex

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens is put 10 cm from a light source and it makes a sharp image on a screen, kept 10 cm from the lens. Now a glass block (refractive index 1.5) of 1.5 cm thickness is placed in between the light source and the lens. To get the sharp image again, the screen is shifted by a distance d. Then d is:
question: A convex lens is put 10 cm from a light source and it makes a sharp image on a screen, kept 10 cm from the lens. Now a glass block (refractive index 1.5) of 1.5 cm thickness is placed in between the light source and the lens. To get the sharp image again, the screen is shifted by a distance d. Then d is:
solution:
From given data

1v-1u=1f

110-1(-10)=1f

f=5cm

When glass plate is placed, shift in object for lens

s=t1-1μ=1.51-23

=0.5 cm

Shift will be in the direction of incoming rays

∴u=10-0.5=9.5 cm

1v-1u=1f

1v=15+1(-9.5)

v=47.54.5 cm

Shift =47.54.5-10=2.54.5=59cm=0.55 cm away from lens.

subject: Physics
q:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length 18 cm in air is immersed in water. The change in focal length of the lens will be_____cm. 

(Given refractive index of water=43)
question:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length 18 cm in air is immersed in water. The change in focal length of the lens will be_____cm. 

(Given refractive index of water=43)
solution:
From Lens Maker's formula,

For air: 1fa=μgμa-1 1R1-1R2

For water: 1fw=μgμw-1 1R1-1R2

∴    1fa1fw=μgμa-1μgμw-1

⇒fwfa=1.5-11.5×34-1⇒fw=0.50.125×18=72 cm

Thus, change in focal length of the lens is 72-18=54 cm

subject: Physics
q: An upright object is placed at a distance of <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in front of a convergent lens of focal length <math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> A convergent mirror of focal length <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at a distance of <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on the other side of the lens. The position and size of the final image will be:
question: An upright object is placed at a distance of <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in front of a convergent lens of focal length <math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> A convergent mirror of focal length <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at a distance of <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> on the other side of the lens. The position and size of the final image will be:
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> from the convergent lens, twice the size of the object
<math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the convergent mirror, twice the size of the object

<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> from the convergent lens, same size of the object

<math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> from the convergent mirror, same size of the object
solution:
First refraction, u=-40 cm; f=+20 cm
From lens formula, 1v-1u=1f
1v-1-40=120⇒1v=120-140
v=+40 cm
m1=-1
First reflection, u=-20 cm;f=-10 cm
From mirror formula, 1v+1u=1f
1v-120=1-10
1v=1-10+120⇒v=-20 cm
m2=-1
Second refraction, u=-40 cm,  f=+20 cm
From lens formula, 1v-1-40=120⇒v=+40 cm
m3=-1
mtotal=m1×m2×m3=-1
Finally, image will be at distance 40 cm  from lens and is of same size as object.

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light is incident at an angle of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mn>60</mn></mrow> <mo>∘</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> on one face of a prism of angle <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mn>30</mn></mrow> <mo>∘</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math>. The emergent ray of light makes an angle of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mn>30</mn></mrow> <mo>∘</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> with incident ray. The angle made by the emergent ray with second face of prism will be:
question: A ray of light is incident at an angle of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mn>60</mn></mrow> <mo>∘</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> on one face of a prism of angle <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mn>30</mn></mrow> <mo>∘</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math>. The emergent ray of light makes an angle of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mn>30</mn></mrow> <mo>∘</mo> </msup> </mrow> </math> with incident ray. The angle made by the emergent ray with second face of prism will be:
solution:
Incidence angle on one face of a prism, i=60°. Angle of the prism, A=30°,

the mergent ray that makes an angle 30° with incident ray. We know, deviation angle, δ= Angle between the incident ray and emergent ray=30°

⇒δ=i+e-A
⇒30°=60°+e−30∘
⇒e=0°. So, the angle with the face, =90° .

subject: Physics
q:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length $f=18 \mathrm{~cm}$ is immersed in water. The difference in focal lengths of the given lens when it is in water and in air is $\alpha \times \mathrm{f}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(refractive index of water $=4 / 3$)
question:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length $f=18 \mathrm{~cm}$ is immersed in water. The difference in focal lengths of the given lens when it is in water and in air is $\alpha \times \mathrm{f}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(refractive index of water $=4 / 3$)
solution:
Using the lensmaker's equation, in air:
$\frac{1}{f_{air}} = (n - 1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$ where $(n-1) = 0.5$ for glass with $n = 1.5$.
In water:
$\frac{1}{f_{water}} = \left(\frac{n}{n_{water}} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
$= \left(\frac{1.5}{4/3} - 1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
$= \frac{1}{8}\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
Since $\left(\frac{1}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_2}\right) = \frac{2}{f_{air}} = \frac{2}{18}$,
We get $\frac{1}{f_{water}} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{2}{18} = \frac{1}{72}$, so $f_{water} = 72$ cm.
The difference is $f_{water} - f_{air} = 72 - 18 = 54$ cm.
Therefore $\alpha = \frac{54}{18} = 3$.
